name: Product designer research-driven input — tests data preservation and tone differentiation

input.userMessage: TeamPulse is a team collab platform. Research (n=24): #1 frustration 'can't find what I need' (71%). #2 notification overload (58%). Behavioral: avg session 12min, 73% start with feed, wiki users 2.4x retention. Primary CTA = content creation (activation: first pulse within 48hrs). User language: 'updates' not 'posts', 'team space' not 'workspace', 'docs' not 'wiki'. Tested formal vs friendly tone — friendly won 83%. Humor in empty states, never in errors.

expectedBehaviors: Terminology maps user language: post→update, workspace→team space, wiki article→doc
contentVoice reflects friendly tone per A/B test result
content-guide.md differentiates empty state tone (humor OK) from error state (no humor)
users.primary.frustrations include search/discovery and notification overload with data
designDecisions captures activation metric (first pulse within 48hrs) as a design driver
Web research supplements the user research, does not override it
antiPatterns: Discards or ignores quantitative research data (percentages, durations)
Overrides user-researched terminology with generic alternatives
Does not differentiate empty state tone from error state tone in content-guide
Treats activation metric as trivia rather than a design driver
Produces a content-guide.md that references none of the research findings
Web research results override user's stated research findings

evaluationCriteria.dataPreservation: Do all 7 quantitative data points appear in the output?
evaluationCriteria.terminologyFromResearch: Does terminology match user-researched language, not product language?
evaluationCriteria.toneDifferentiation: Does content-guide differentiate humor policy by context (empty vs error)?
evaluationCriteria.researchAsDriver: Is the activation metric treated as a design decision, not background info?
